Tweet_Text:
President Ruto keeps boasting that 27 million Kenyans have been registered under the Social Health Authority (SHA) — and yes, that sounds impressive on paper. But what he’s not telling us is the real story: only around 5 million Kenyans are actively contributing.

That means over 22 million Kenyans — the majority — are registered in name only. They can’t access SHA services when they get sick, because registration without contribution means exclusion.

So let’s be honest: what’s the point of registering millions if they can’t benefit? Health insurance isn’t about numbers on a database — it’s about access, fairness, and affordability.

Instead of chasing statistics for headlines, the government should focus on creating jobs, improving incomes, and making contributions realistic for ordinary Kenyans. Otherwise, SHA risks becoming another grand promise with no real impact on the ground.

Tweet_Text:
The Roche–MoH–SHA MOU Implementation Workshop
17th November 2025, marked our Day 1 of the Roche–MoH–SHA MOU Implementation Workshop at the Great Rift Valley Lodge with a high-level opening plenary bringing together leadership from @MOH_Kenya,  @NCIKenya, @_shakenya, @Kemsa_Kenya, @DHA_Kenya the MoH Division of Health Products & Technologies (DHPT), @Roche, and Savannah Global Health Institute, @SGHInstitute. The day set a unified tone for strengthening Kenya’s national architecture for breast and cervical cancer control.

Sessions highlighted the current state of breast and cervical cancer in Kenya, reviewing epidemiological trends and the health system burden. Partners explored the investment case for oncology in Africa, followed by a review of health financing achievements and challenges from the previous MOU—now reframed within SHA’s expanded mandate and DHA’s emerging digital ecosystem for claims, approvals, and health data integration.

KEMSA and DHPT shared key lessons from past collaborations in supply chain optimisation, commodity access, and regulatory alignment, while Roche provided insights on partnerships for access to innovation. SGHI contributed perspectives on systems strengthening, implementation support, and global standards that can fortify national cancer pathways.

The afternoon was dedicated to a deep-dive workshop to co-develop clear Terms of Reference and KPIs for the new MOU. Stakeholders identified actionable priorities in screening, diagnostics, treatment access, sample referral systems, supply chain efficiency, digital health integration, and sustainable financing across all levels of care.
Day 1 concluded with a consolidation of emerging priorities and agreement on action points, establishing a strong foundation for the technical workstreams scheduled for Day 2.

Kenya stands at a point where excuses are exhausted and patience has run out. 

The cost of corruption is no longer abstract it is visible in the hospitals without medicine, the youth without jobs, the roads that never get completed, and the public money that disappears without consequence. 

A nation cannot grow where theft becomes routine and leadership becomes performance instead of service.

It is a call to move from survival politics to systems politics. Komboa Kenya Stephen Kalonzo Musyoka.

Tweet_Text:
Fellow Kenyans,

William Ruto says he is passionate about the health of this nation. Yet thousands of Kenyans know a different reality.

Does he know how many patients are detained in public hospitals because they cannot clear their medical bills? Does he know how many families cannot bury their loved ones because bodies remain held in mortuaries over unpaid fees?

Does he know the suffering of cancer patients who spend months moving from one facility to another in search of treatment? 

Does he know what ordinary Kenyans go through when they walk into public hospitals only to find medicine unavailable and critical services overstretched?

When patients were stranded at Mama Lucy Hospital over unpaid bills, it was well-wishers and leaders stepping in to help desperate families. That is the reality many Kenyans face every day.

A president who claims to understand the struggles of the people must first acknowledge the crisis facing our healthcare system. 

Leadership is not about speeches, photo opportunities, or public relations. It is about ensuring that no Kenyan is denied treatment because they are poor.

The true test of leadership is whether the sick can access healthcare, whether hospitals are equipped, whether medicine is available, and whether families can afford treatment without being pushed into poverty.

Kenyans deserve honesty. They deserve functioning hospitals. They deserve dignity. Above all, they deserve leaders who understand the reality they live in every day.

There has been tangible economic development in the country as a result of government investment, and it has had a substantial impact on the healthcare structure in the country. 

This is evidenced by the improved healthcare access achieved through the implementation of UHC and NHIF reforms, as well as the enhanced infrastructure from the upgrading of hospitals and clinics, and free maternity services which has led to better maternal and infant care. 

While there has been digitization and telemedicine in the healthcare sector that has improved efficiency, the health workforce has grown, ensuring that there are more medical personnel in rural areas. 

There are now more available essential medicines and immunization programs, and dependency on foreign treatment has decreased due to specialized care services for chronic diseases.

 There has also been improved responsiveness by the emergency reponce systems that are able to provide medical assistance in a shorter timeframe. 

The combined efforts have resulted in strengthened health care in Kenya, enabling citizens to have better health outcomes.

We had the honour of hosting Prof. Dr. Muhandule Birindwa Archippe from Bukavu in Eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), who visited us this morning at Dr. Kalebi Labs (DKL) 🇰🇪🫱🏾‍🫲🏽🇨🇩

Prof. Archippe is the Medical Director of Bukavu Hospital and also a Professor at the Official University of Bukavu. With extensive experience in paediatrics, academic medicine, clinical leadership and public health research across Central Africa, his visit carried significant regional importance.

He visited DKL specifically to explore regional referral laboratory collaboration and access to specialised and esoteric diagnostic testing services for the Eastern DRC region, where many institutions still face major challenges associated with sending samples to Europe and South Africa - including very high costs and prolonged turnaround times.

Prof. Archippe was accompanied by Ms. Linda Muringo, CEO of Excella Biotechnologies Limited - our regional scientific partner and supplier of the Beckman Coulter Flow Cytometry solutions deployed at DKL. These include the fully automated Beckman Coulter Aquios platform for CD4/CD8 testing as well as the advanced DxFLEX flow cytometer, which DKL pioneered in the region for advanced leukemia & lymphoma immunophenotyping using the highly sophisticated FDA-approved ClearLLab 10C panels that enable highly accurate, standardized and guideline-driven diagnosis of hematologic malignancies.

Beyond flow cytometry, Prof. Archippe toured our broad spectrum of advanced diagnostic platforms and was particularly impressed by the depth and sophistication of the DKL test menu — including Next Generation Sequencing (NGS), molecular diagnostics, FISH, electrophoresis, immunohistochemistry, digital pathology workflows, and the first of its kind in Africa unique Total Laboratory Automation (TLA) setup. He especially marvelled at our extensive autoimmune and allergy testing portfolio running on advanced Chemiluminescence Immunoassay (ChLIA) and immunoblot platforms.

What truly stood out was his heartfelt remark that despite having worked in several European countries including France and Sweden, DKL was “the most sophisticated laboratory” he had ever seen anywhere - something that made him immensely proud to witness right here in Africa.

It was indeed a proud moment for our homegrown ultramodern laboratory venture and deeply meaningful because such remarks reinforce the growing reality that world-class, highly sophisticated diagnostic medicine can truly be built right here in Africa - by Africans, for Africa.

We are truly grateful for the visit and the rich discussions, and we look forward to deeper engagement with Prof. Archippe and Bukavu Hospital as we continue strengthening DKL’s role as a regional referral laboratory of excellence proudly serving the wider East African Community - including the DRC, now an important member of the EAC regional bloc.

#DKL #DrKalebiLabs #EastAfrica #DRC #EAC #Pathology #FlowCytometry #MolecularDiagnostics #NGS #DigitalPathology

This morning, I had the pleasure of hosting Dr. Ally Abdulsaam Bajser, Consultant Urologist and Endourology Specialist from Dar es Salaam, Tanzania, who made the trip all the way to see me Dr. Kalebi Labs (DKL) on order to discuss medical entrepreneurship, healthcare innovation and explore potential laboratory partnership opportunities.

Dr. Ally Bajser is a highly driven specialist with a vision of establishing a one-of-a-kind private specialized urology center in Tanzania, and I truly appreciated his motivation, focus, energy, and passion for advancing healthcare within the region.

It was inspiring showing him around DKL and taking him through our advanced diagnostic ecosystem - including our Total Laboratory Automation (TLA), fully digital pathology workflow, molecular diagnostics, flow cytometry, next generation sequencing (NGS), allergy and autoimmune testing, automated microbiology, DNA paternity testing and other specialized platforms that have positioned DKL as a leading regional referral laboratory now 1½ years from inception in October 2024.

We held a truly insightful and productive discussion around specialist practice, diagnostics, innovation, entrepreneurship, and the future of homegrown African healthcare institutions and collaborations.

Grateful for the visit and the wonderful exchange of ideas. Thank you very much, Dr. Ally Bajser, for taking the time to visit us. I look forward to insha’Allah building strong future engagements and collaborations that will continue advancing healthcare innovation and regional partnerships across East Africa.

#DKL #DrKalebiLabs #MedicalEntrepreneurship #MedicalPartnerships

Dr Gikonyo’s testimony brings the presidency directly into the private medical space of a political rival at a moment of intense national tension.

According to the court record President William Ruto personally contacted the cardiologist handling Rigathi Gachagua’s treatment at Karen Hospital to inquire about his condition while he was still undergoing tests for chest complications during impeachment proceedings. 

The doctor stated that he provided updates on the ongoing assessments after first obtaining the patient’s consent.

This detail places the highest office in direct contact with sensitive medical information about a key political opponent during a period of serious political conflict. 

It raises questions about how far state attention extends into private health matters when politics is at its peak.

The situation becomes more complicated with the court revealing concerns over missing medical records. 

The doctor admitted that several scans laboratory results and imaging documents were not included in the affidavit presented before the court.

What remains is a record that combines political tension medical privacy and gaps in documentation all unfolding at the same time in a highly charged national moment.

Tweet_Text:
Let us be honest: the issue is not that medical interns are too many. 
It is not that Uganda has no money. 
It is that medical interns are not being treated as a priority.

Consider the choices being made:

• Parliament keeps growing.
Parliament’s budget reportedly doubled to about 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟭. 𝟮 𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗼𝗻. The money going to 529 MPs rose from about 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟰𝟬𝟬𝗯 in 2020/21 to 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟳𝟰𝟰.𝟰𝗯 in 2026/27, an increase of about 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟯𝟰𝟰.𝟰𝗯. 
What direct return does this give the common Ugandan in a crowded hospital?

• Two offices alone tell the story.
The Speaker and Deputy Speaker offices had about 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟳.𝟭𝗯 combined in 2020/21. In 2026/27, they stand at about 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟱𝟬.𝟮𝗯 , an increase of about 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟰𝟯.𝟭𝗯 for only two offices.
That increase alone can pay 𝟯𝟬𝟬𝟬 interns 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟭𝗺 𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗵 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗮 𝗳𝘂𝗹𝗹 𝘆𝗲𝗮𝗿, with money left.

• Questionable spending continues.
In 2025/26, selected Speaker’s office lines reportedly included 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟮.𝟰𝗯 for foreign travel, 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟵𝟲𝟲𝗺 for fuel, 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟰.𝟴𝗯 for incapacity, death benefits and funeral expenses, and 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟱.𝟮𝗯 for donations. Total: about 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟭𝟰.𝟮𝗯. What lasting public health return does this produce compared with doctors on wards?

• RDC structures are being funded.
Uganda reportedly has 146 RDCs, 170 Deputy RDCs and 432 Assistant RDCs, total 748 officials. Their proposed salary enhancement requires an extra 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟮𝟵.𝟬𝟳𝟵𝗯 every year. 
Add the reported 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟯𝟬𝗯 for LC I to LC V political leader facilitation, and that is about 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟱𝟵𝗯. In what way does this benefit the common Ugandan? 

• Donations are funded.
State House donations reportedly consumed 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟳𝟱𝟭𝗯 over seven financial years. In 2023/24 alone, donations were budgeted at 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟭𝟴.𝟭𝗯, but actual spending reached 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟴𝟬.𝟭𝟴𝗯. If tens and hundreds of billions can be found for donations, how does 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟮𝟰𝗯 to 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟯𝟲𝗯 for over 2,000 medical interns become impossible?

• Health was not protected with the same urgency.
The Ministry of Health vote fell from about 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟭. 𝟲𝟵𝟯 𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗼𝗻 in FY2023/24 to about 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟭. 𝟯𝟰𝟰 𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗼𝗻 in FY2024/25, a reduction of about  𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟯𝟰𝟵𝗯. Even the 2025/26 estimate of 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟭.𝟱𝟲𝟰 𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗼𝗻 remains below the 2023/24 level. Yet health is the sector that directly touches mothers in labour, accident victims, children with malaria, emergency patients and families in public hospitals.

Now compare:

• 2,000 interns × Shs1m × 12 months =  𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟮𝟰𝗯 per year
• 2,500 interns × Shs1m × 12 months =  𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟯𝟬𝗯 per year
• 3,000 interns × Shs1m × 12 months = 𝗦𝗵𝘀 𝟯𝟲𝗯 per year

Even using the Ministry of Health’s own gross figure of Shs15.6m per intern per year, the reported 2,706 eligible interns would require about Shs42.2b. That is still small compared with what is being found for political comfort and administrative expansion.

That money is not a handout.

✨ It avails doctors on wards.
✨ It keeps emergency units covered.
✨ It supports maternity care.
✨ It fills staffing gaps in regional referrals.
✨ It protects patients.

So let us stop pretending.

This is not a numbers problem.
This is not a money problem.
It is a priority problem.

Medical interns are doctors under apprenticeship, not free labour! 

#InternsNotSlaves

Tweet_Text:
Y’all remember when they arrested us in December 2021 and I almost fell off a speeding police pickup? Yes, that time medical interns were on strike decrying low pay among other poor welfare issues, and on this particular day we were arrested while peacefully walking to Parliament to deliver our petition. Yes, at that time we were being paid 700k per person and we demanded an increase in pay.

And following a prolonged strike that took about six weeks, the government responded and increased our pay from 700k to 2.5M monthly. But this change was implemented for only two years, then without any discussions with stakeholders they reduced it to 1M and now they have completely eliminated it.

This leaves me with so many questions? Does the government even know why they pay interns? I doubt. Do they even know why they increased our pay then only to take it back a few years later? Do they…. ???? 🤦‍♀️

Please stop politicizing everything. When we say pay interns we mean prioritize the health of Ugandans. 📌

#PayAllMedicalInterns #SaveLives

This Heroes Day, we bow in honour of those who gave their lives in the line of duty, shielding our people from Ebola, Covid 19, and other deadly diseases.

Because they stood in danger, many still live. Families survived. Communities endured. Uganda still stands.

When a Tooth Infection Turns Deadly: A Story from Koome Islands

I joined friends and family of the late Rev. Ken Nyagudi at All Saints Cathedral, Nairobi this evening, as they made final preparations to honour his inspiring legacy. The former Kisumu West MP passed away on Sunday, April 5, while being treated at Kenyatta National Hospital's private wing.

I was joined by National Assembly Speaker  @HonWetangula, Nairobi County Senator @edwinsifuna and Embakasi South MP @MMawathe.

I served with Mheshimiwa Nyagudi in Parliament when he was elected in a 2004 by-election following the death of Hon Joab Omino in January of the same year up to 2007. He was a remarkable man: humble and selfless. His record as a legislator was impeccable. Mheshimiwa Nyagudi always offered to lead our praise and worship sessions, and I held him in the highest esteem. 

May Mheshimiwa Ken Nyagudi's soul rest in eternal peace.

NCI-K Leadership Marks World Cancer Day with KNH Visit and AI-Focused Cancer Workshop
In commemoration of World Cancer Day, the NCI-K Board Chair, Dr. Timothy Olweny, accompanied the Principal Secretary for Health, Dr. Oluga Ouma, on a visit to Kenyatta National Hospital (KNH) where they toured the oncology clinic and interacted with cancer patients. The visit offered a valuable opportunity to listen directly to patients and better understand the challenges they face in accessing and receiving care. The KNH CEO was also present.
They later joined a workshop on “Harnessing Data and AI to Strengthen Cancer Prevention, Diagnosis, and Care in Kenya,” which focused on AI applications in cancer imaging. Representatives made presentations from the University of Michigan Center for Global Health Equity, University of Nairobi, Aga Khan University, KNH, and NCI-K.
While addressing participants, Dr. Olweny emphasized the need for healthcare providers to demonstrate greater empathy in patient care. He also highlighted the high number of cancer-related deaths and called for stronger collective efforts to curb the burden of the disease.
On his part, Dr. Oluga Ouma urged members of the public to prioritize early cancer screening, noting that early detection significantly improves treatment outcomes. He also called on medical practitioners to uphold professionalism and strengthen early detection practices to avoid late diagnoses.

Exactly 2 years ago today - 16th May 2024 📸 We were right in the middle of construction works at Real Towers Annexe at what would soon become the home of Dr. Kalebi Labs (DKL) next to KNH in Kibra sub-county⏳ 

At that stage, it was still mostly bare concrete floors, exposed pillars, loose cables, ongoing screed concrete layering and curing ahead of floor tiling as we prepared for the next phase of mechanical works 🏗️

Looking back at those photos feels quite surreal knowing back then the vision taking shape 🔭 

Barely four months later, we had completed the setup, obtained full registration and licensing as Kenya’s first private Class G National & International Referral Laboratory, and successfully launched a first-of-its-kind Total Lab Automation (TLA) system integrating chemistry, immunogenicity and haematology⌛️

Today, just two years down the line @DKLDrKalebiLabs has grown into:
▪️ A proudly homegrown ultramodern referral lab serving Kenya and the wider region
▫️ Africa’s first fully digital pathology workflow and paperless clinical lab
▪️ Advanced molecular diagnostics, NGS-based oncogenetics, in-house flow cytometry & AI-powered lab systems
▫️ ISO 15189 accreditation and CAP Proficiency
▪️ Licensed Level 4B Specialized Medical Centre providing SHA specialised tests
▫️ … and much more, in our 24/7 central referral laboratory delivering world-class diagnostics

From screed cementing to a leading homegrown one-of-a-kind ultramodern state-of-the-art diagnostic powerhouse -  Alhamdulillah!

The sheer amount of work, sacrifice, risk, pressure, persistence, faith and teamwork that goes into building something so pioneering and meaningful to this scale from absolutely nothing has been amazing.

I’m deeply grateful to every single person who has been part of the DKL journey from the very beginning - from my amazing team at the lab, to investment financing partners, contractors, suppliers, regulators, mentors, friends, family, and all those who supported us long before the vision became visible to everyone else.

A New Era of Lab Services 🩸🌙🌱

#DKL #DrKalebiLabs #FromVisionToReality #ProudlyKenyan

Tweet_Text:
These past two days have felt surreal and eerily quiet at our Dr. Kalebi Labs (DKL) central referral laboratory in Real Towers Annexe next to KNH in Upper Hill.

The transport strike transformed our usually buzzing 24/7 facility into what genuinely felt like a ghost lab - only skeleton staff at their workstations, drastically reduced patient footfall, and an unusual stillness across a place normally defined by nonstop movement and activity.

Out of our 110+ team members, fewer than 20% managed to physically make it in, for whom we had to specially organize transport just to maintain essential services and operational continuity.

Yet remarkably, the work never stopped. 

Samples continued pouring in from across Nairobi and beyond, while the few drive-in patients who made it to the facility were still attended to. Many team members also powered through remotely from home, making our digital pathology systems and remote connectivity capabilities incredibly valuable during this period.

In fact, the relative quiet even created an opportunity to push ahead with QA activities, audits, validations, and other important behind-the-scenes work that is often difficult to fully focus on during peak operational intensity. Still, I genuinely missed the usual hustle and bustle that characterizes DKL at full throttle.

It is a huge relief that the strike has now been called off. I sincerely hope the next 7 days of negotiations deliver a practical and sustainable solution around fuel prices, because the impact on healthcare, businesses, and ordinary livelihoods has been immense.

Time now to reset and continue advancing our mission in the New Era of Lab Services. 🩸🌙🌱

Tweet_Text:
A Kenyan walks into Mama Lucy Hospital sick and desperate for help. The doctor says an endoscopy is needed. But it cannot be done there. The patient is referred to KNH where they are told to pay KSh 20,000 from their own pocket because SHA does not cover it.

After struggling to raise that money, they are told to wait THREE MONTHS because the queue is too long. Then comes the real shock: “If you can afford it, go to a private hospital.” Some are even directed to specific hospitals by the same people inside KNH.

So what exactly are Kenyans paying taxes for? What is SHA helping if a sick citizen still has to choose between death, debt or humiliation?

Kibaki found children out of school and fought to put them back into classrooms. Uhuru made education affordable enough for millions of families to survive. Today under William Ruto over 180,000 children are out of school.

Those children do not disappear. They end up in the streets. Crime rises. Teen pregnancies rise. Child labour rises. Entire futures are buried while the government is busy with luxury convoys, PR tours and political games.

A nation that abandons its children and sick citizens is not developing. It is collapsing slowly while leaders celebrate statistics and empty slogans.

Kenyans, we made a mistake. But mistakes can be corrected by brave people who refuse to surrender their country. 2027 must not just be an election. It must be liberation. A united people is stronger than propaganda, stronger than tribal division and stronger than fear.

This country belongs to wananchi, not cartels feeding on pain and poverty.

Nakuru County has launched an in-house Optical Laboratory at Nakuru County Referral and Teaching Hospital, becoming only the second public health facility in Kenya after Kenyatta National Hospital to offer local spectacle production services.

Established through a partnership involving the Ministry of Health, Nakuru County Government and development partners, the facility will produce up to 500 prescription spectacles daily, reducing costs and waiting times for patients. 

The laboratory is expected to improve access to affordable eye care services for residents of Nakuru and neighbouring counties while strengthening specialized healthcare and advancing Universal Health Coverage.

Tweet_Text:
The Government of Kenya is demonstrating strong national ownership and fiscal discipline in the health sector through the 2025 Resource Mapping and Tracking (RMT) initiative. Led by the Ministry of Health, this first comprehensive, government-driven exercise improves transparency of both domestic and external health financing and strengthens the evidence base for sustainable resource allocation.

Domestic health financing is projected to increase from KES 95.6 billion in FY 2022/23 to KES 164.9 billion by FY 2027/28, signaling a clear response to declining donor support. The enactment of the Social Health Insurance, Primary Healthcare, and Digital Health Acts in 2023 provides a solid legal foundation for Universal Health Coverage.

Equally important is the Ministry’s strengthened stewardship, coordinating 27 external partners and aligning resources with national priorities, while increasing domestic investment in core system functions such as governance, infrastructure, and health financing reforms.

​#GlobalHealth #KenyaHealth #UHC #SustainableFinancing #HealthSystems  @UNAIDS @UNAIDS_ESA

Tweet_Text:
Yesterday , I spent the greater part of the day at the Ministry of Health, Rwanda Headquarters [@RwandaHealth] with other MPA Pioneers from the African School of Governance. Learning how the health system in Rwanda functions right from community health workers at the grassroots to the national referral hospitals that enabled me to make the connection between policy and actual service delivery.

We were welcomed by the Minister of State for Health Hon. Dr. Yvan Butera [@YvanButera]
who talked about the architecture of the health system in Rwanda. I asked about the increasing challenge of non-communicable diseases such as diabetes on the rise and he stressed that prevention must go beyond the hospital walls. He mentioned the role of the media in educating the public about healthy living, early screening as well as awareness reminding us that informed citizens are the first line of defense in protecting public health.

As a communication professional, his emphasis on embracing technology as a way of increasing efficiency was a strong reminder that media and technology are not just enablers but crucial pillars in constructing a responsive, preventive and people-centered health system.

📷 By @RwandaHealth

Tweet_Text:
Today, the Integrated Model for Access, Retention, and Advanced Breast Cancer Care (IMARA–BC) was officially launched at Afya House in Nairobi by AMPATH Kenya in collaboration with the Ministry of Health Kenya and with support from The Pfizer Foundation, this is a three-year initiative aimed at expanding access to timely diagnosis, treatment, and survivorship support for women across the country.
The Cabinet Secretary for Health, Aden Duale, commended partners for advancing a coordinated and patient-centred approach that strengthens breast cancer care across the full continuum — from awareness and early detection to diagnosis, treatment, survivorship, and national policy alignment.
Through support from The Pfizer Foundation, the initiative will expand digital diagnostics and screening services, strengthen patient navigation and referral systems, build the capacity of Community Health Promoters and healthcare providers, enhance national cancer data systems, and promote coordinated stakeholder engagement for long-term sustainability.
IMARA–BC forms part of The Pfizer Foundation’s global Action & Impact programme addressing breast cancer disparities across sub-Saharan Africa, including Kenya, Ethiopia, Ghana, Rwanda, and Tanzania.
Working alongside partners such as Moi Teaching and Referral Hospital, Moi University, and the National Cancer Control Programme, the initiative will advance timely, high-quality breast cancer care for women across Kenya.
The National Cancer Institute of Kenya CEO, Dr. Elias Melly, attended the launch and reaffirmed the Institute’s commitment to strengthening cancer prevention, early detection, treatment coordination, research, and policy alignment in Kenya. As the national agency mandated to coordinate cancer control, NCI-K will play a key role in supporting data systems strengthening, stakeholder coordination, and integration of breast cancer services within the broader National Cancer Control framework.
The launch was attended by Principal Secretary for Public Health and Professional Standards Mary Muthoni, Director General for Health Patrick Amoth, CEO of Moi Teaching and Referral Hospital Philip Kirwa, CEO of KEMSA Erjesa Waqo, and representatives from AMPATH, and NCCP.
Together, these partnerships signal a renewed national commitment to reducing breast cancer mortality and ensuring that no woman is left behind in accessing quality cancer care.

To enhance access to quality healthcare services for residents of Kalama Ward, I have officially commissioned Makakoi Dispensary.

I just can't wish medical internship on my worst enemy and let alone without allowances.

Tweet_Text: In the streets of Nanyuki, a solitary protester appeared — not in the usual jeans and T-shirt, but sealed head-to-toe in full white protective gear, the kind used for handling Ebola patients. In their hands, a simple wooden cross bearing two stark words: "REJECT EBOLA."No chants. No speeches. Just a silent, haunting figure moving through the town.This was no ordinary protest. It was performance art that cut straight through the noise. The hazmat suit declared: “This is what you want us to wear.” The cross answered: “This is the burden you’re forcing us to carry.” Together, they formed both a prayer and a warning, wrapped in sterile white plastic.Nanyuki has marched. It has held placards. It has raised the voices of grandmothers and boda boda riders. Now it speaks through a lone ghost in white — a living image of the future this town refuses to accept.The message is unmistakable: Nanyuki does not need hazmat suits for Ebola.

Tweet_Text:
As a Senior Consultant, the reason I haven't raised my voice is simple:
When the interns are gone, I will gladly show up at 5AM, clerk 80 patients, draw the blood, and run the night calls myself.
I am superhuman. Obviously.

As a Senior Nursing Officer, the reason I am silent is obvious:
I have no problem running three wards alone, fixing lines, tracking vitals, delivering babies, doing the paperwork.
I don't need hands. I have dedication.

As a Policymaker, the reason I haven't spoken is elegant:
The interns are a budget problem I solved by terming the students.
My children are not doing internship in Uganda, after all.

As a Patient, the reason I haven't complained is clear:
Even if the doctor cutting me open has worked 36 hours without food, just cut me open and take the baby out.
Hunger sharpens the hands. Everyone knows this.

As a Citizen, the reason I am unbothered is rational:
None of my children is a medic.
I have my pastor.
The system runs on miracles. Always has.

This policy is brilliant.
Let's all stay quiet and watch the magic happen.

The Uganda Prisons Service received a major boost to its healthcare system with the donation of a modern Digital X-Ray (DXR CAD) machine at Luzira Staff Health Facility. The Ministry of Health handed over the equipment in a ceremony attended by Ms. Stella Nabunya, Commissioner of Prisons (Health Services) who represented the Commissioner General of Prisons. This new, portable X-ray machine, supported by trained prison staff, will improve healthcare for inmates and staff across the country by making it easier to diagnose and treat illnesses.

The DXR CAD machine, donated through the Ministry of Health’s National Tuberculosis and Leprosy Program (NTLP) with help from the United States Government, is part of a project that provided 48 portable X-ray machines to improve screening for tuberculosis (TB) and other lung diseases. This mobile machine, based at Luzira, will serve not only the prison but also other prisons and nearby communities, making healthcare more accessible. 

The X-ray machine is a big step forward for healthcare in Uganda’s prisons, where it’s often hard to get quick and accurate medical care. Because it’s portable and operated by trained prison staff, it can be used in many places, helping both inmates and staff across the country.

The DXR CAD machine takes clear images, helping doctors spot health problems like TB or lung issues accurately. This means fewer mistakes and faster treatment. 2. **Healthcare on the Move**: The machine can be moved to different prisons, so even those in far-off areas can get X-ray services without traveling to distant hospitals.

The machine is perfect for finding TB early, especially in high-risk groups like prisoners or people with HIV. Trained prison staff can use it to screen people quickly and start treatment sooner. The machine makes it quicker for doctors to check and treat patients, reducing waiting times and helping prison healthcare staff work more efficiently. Two workers from Luzira—a radiographer and a clinician—have been trained to use the machine. More staff will learn through training sessions, ensuring the machine is used well in many prisons.

Ms. Stella Nabunya thanked the Ministry of Health and NTLP for the donation, saying it will greatly improve healthcare in prisons. “This machine will help us take better care of inmates and staff, not just at Luzira but across Uganda,” she said. The event was attended by key figures like Dr. Kisambu, Assistant Commissioner of Health Services; Mr. Xavier Elubu, Assistant Commissioner of Prisons; Mr. Thomas Apila, Officer In Charge of Luzira Staff Hospital; Dr. Jingo, Medical Superintendent; and NTLP representatives Dr. Joshua Muhumuza, Dr. Kaggwa Charles, and Dr. Jjingo Erisa Mukisa.

With proper planning and documentation, it can be used for TB screening in prisons and nearby communities, especially in areas with high TB cases. The Ministry of Health has set clear guidelines, including following safety rules from the Atomic Energy Council and maintaining the machine properly to ensure it lasts. By focusing on high-risk groups and using trained prison staff, the Uganda Prisons Service can catch health problems early and treat them effectively. This will make a big difference in the health of inmates and staff across the country.

Can. Dr. Johnson Byabashaija, Commissioner General of Prisons, completed a strategic and working tour of Eastern Uganda, visiting Bubulo, Masufu, and Tororo prisons to tackle pressing issues such as prison congestion, healthcare improvements, and staff professional development. Engaging with officers in charge prisons from the Eastern, Iganga, and Southeast regions, he outlined plans to enhance service delivery, promote transparency, and improve conditions within the Uganda Prisons Service (UPS).

During the tour, Dr. Byabashaija opened two new prisoner wards at Musafu Prison in Busia District and Bubulo Prison in Manafwa District. Each ward can accommodate over 100 inmates, helping to ease the strain of overcrowding, which has long impacted service quality.

He directed officers in charge to address overcrowding in the coming months, emphasizing its critical importance in creating safe and rehabilitative environments for inmates.

A major focus of the tour was the enhancement of healthcare services, including the establishment of a new hospital at Luzira equipped with modern tools, such as a recently donated Digital X-Ray (DXR CAD) machine. This portable machine, operated by trained prison staff, will be used across various prisons to improve screening for tuberculosis (TB) and other health conditions, benefiting both inmates and staff nationwide.

Cervical Cancer Symptoms

Early signs:
- Abnormal bleeding (after intimacy, between periods, or after menopause)
- Unusual discharge or pelvic pain

Advanced signs:
- Back pain, leg swelling, or tiredness

See a doctor if you notice these signs. Early detection saves lives!
#CervicalCancerAwareness #KnowTheSigns #ScreeningSavesLives

Did you know?

-Regular exercises help strengthen your body-muscles/brain.
But most importantly, helps fortify your heart & blood vessels.

•Do regular exercises, get your RESTING BLOOD PRESSURE to at most 110/70 mmHg thereabout.

...Get that RESTING PULSE RATE to at most 75 beats per minute, mine ranges at 55-65 at rest.

-If your resting heart rate is always high(>90 beats per minute), something MUST be off.

Get checked by a doctor and do lifestyle changes, popping antihypertensive pills is not enough!

~Drop that sugar, eat right, work out!

$Your Health is your responsibility ✍🏾

#Hypertension #Erectiledysfunction

Tweet_Text:
Kenya has not just lost a doctor, it has lost a brave patriot who refused to kneel before intimidation and abuse of power.

Dr Job Obwaka was not an ordinary man. He was a distinguished gynaecologist, a respected medical leader, and a defender of integrity inside one of Kenya’s most important medical institutions. He dedicated his life to saving lives, strengthening healthcare, and standing for professionalism when many chose silence.

But instead of honouring his service, this regime humiliated him.

William Ruto’s administration arrested him, dragged him through false accusations, detained him, and subjected him to suffering that no innocent Kenyan should ever endure. Even under pressure, threats, and intimidation, Dr Obwaka refused to compromise his principles. He stood firm. He refused to surrender his conscience for political convenience.

Today, Kenya mourns a man whose health deteriorated after the trauma, injuries, and complications linked to detention and persecution.

That stain will not disappear.

The same people who pushed him out with force and intimidation will now shamelessly rush to occupy his space at Nairobi Hospital, pretending to mourn the very man they persecuted.

History will remember the difference.

Dr Job Obwaka stood with dignity, courage, and honour.
His tormentors stood with power, arrogance, and persecution.

Kenya has lost more than a doctor.

It has lost a fearless reformer.

A principled patriot.
A man who chose suffering over surrender.

May his soul rest in peace, and may truth outlive intimidation.

🎖 VICTORY FOR DOCTORS AS #EMBU COUNTY IMPLEMENTS LONG-AWAITED PROMOTIONS AND CONTRACT CONVERSIONS

After years of waiting, we are proud to announce that Embu County has finally promoted its consultants. The County has promoted a total of 19 consultants who for a long time have only dreamed of such progression. Today, that dream has become a reality and stands as a clear testament to our collective power.

In 2021, Embu doctors watched as promotions were announced in other counties while they remained stagnant. However, through consistent follow-ups, strategic media engagement, and years of agitation including two industrial actions, the most recent in December 2025, we have seen results.

To date, over 80 percent of doctors in the County have been promoted in three successive batches. Furthermore, all contracts have been converted to Permanent and Pensionable status and more doctors are being employed. Our focus remains clear: to ensure no doctor is left behind.

We are deeply grateful to the Embu KMPDU members for believing in the Union and heeding our call to action. It is through this unwavering solidarity that we are now bearing fruit. We thank you for remaining united and for being each other’s keeper.

To the branch leadership under the command of our Deputy Secretary General, Dr. @MiskellahMD, who was unjustly terminated but has since been reinstated, promoted to his rightful Job Group, and is awaiting his due arrears, thank you for remaining steadfast and firm. Your uncompromising stand on the issues affecting our members is a testament to what we can achieve as a community of workers when we remain focused and united.

As a Union, we believe there is nothing we cannot achieve when we put our collective might and mind to the task.
We are stronger together.

Tweet_Text:
⏳️ THE TIME FOR #ISIOLO IS NOW: STANDING AGAINST INJUSTICE

We convened a Doctors’ Kamkunji in Isiolo County, standing shoulder to shoulder with our colleagues as they spoke with courage and painful honesty about the injustices imposed on them by the County Government. What is happening on the ground is not merely a breach of labour laws, it is a direct assault on professional dignity and human rights.

We heard disturbing accounts of:

1. Career Stagnation – Promotions delayed or denied for years, eroding morale and professional growth.

2. Medical Exclusion – Doctors without medical cover, unable to access the very services they provide to the public.

3. Financial Sabotage – Non-remittance of statutory deductions—pension, SHA, PAYE, loans, and SACCO—pushing doctors into financial distress.

4. Precarious Employment – Contract terms that openly contravene our Collective Bargaining Agreements (CBAs).

5. A Culture of Fear – Political interference and workplace intimidation undermining professionalism.

6. System Collapse Risk – A critical shortage of doctors, including a situation where a single surgeon remains on call 24/7.

7. Chronic Salary Delays – Persistent late payments causing immense personal and family hardship.

Despite the deep frustration and exhaustion expressed by doctors in Isiolo, there is renewed hope in the strength of union solidarity under the Kenya Medical Practitioners, Pharmacists and Dentists Union. Our members understand that dignity is not granted, it is defended collectively.

These injustices cannot be normalized. The time for patience has expired.

Accordingly, the Union has formally declared a dispute with the Ministry of Labour and Social Protection, and we are citing the County Government for contempt of court for failing to honour the Return-to-Work Formula (RTWF) of May 8, 2024. We are now moving in deliberate and lawful escalation toward a full industrial strike in Isiolo County.

We shall leave no doctor behind.
The time for Isiolo is now.

We are stronger together.

This young girl swallowed a coin last December, and after months of complications, doctors scheduled surgery to remove it.

Let’s strip the propaganda and look at raw numbers:

NHIF in 2023 collected KSh 82B and paid out KSh 68B. That’s an 83% payout not perfect, but money was actually reaching hospitals and patients.

SHIF in 2025 collected KSh 58B then paid out KSh 92B. A 159% payout.

Read that again.

They are paying out far more than they collect. That’s not efficiency. That’s a financial time bomb.

Either
The system is bleeding through fraud and mismanagement
It’s being artificially propped up and will eventually collapse
There are hidden debts Kenyans will be forced to cover later

You don’t replace a functioning system with one that can’t sustain itself unless the goal isn’t healthcare, but control of the money.

Kenyans were promised a better, more efficient health fund. What we’re seeing instead is a structure that cannot survive without draining the public.

This isn’t reform. It’s a downgrade dressed in big promises.

Tweet_Text:
Since Ruto took office, Kenyans have been told the solution to every problem is... another deduction.

📈 Housing Levy: 0% ➜ 1.5% of gross salary.
🏥 Health Insurance (SHIF): NHIF's fixed contributions ➜ 2.75% of gross salary.
⛽ Fuel VAT: 8% ➜ 16% (doubled in 2023).
🏢 Turnover Tax: 1% ➜ 3% for qualifying businesses.
💰 PAYE: Top rate 30% ➜ 35% for higher earners.
🏠 Housing Levy + SHIF alone: 0% ➜ 4.25% of gross income deducted before you've even bought a loaf of bread.

Meanwhile: 
❓ Has your cost of living gone down? ❓ Are roads dramatically better? 
❓ Is healthcare noticeably better? 
❓ Do you feel richer than you did in 2022? 
❓ Have public services improved in proportion to what you're paying?

At what point do citizens stop being taxpayers and become shareholders demanding a return on investment?

Have you gotten value for money since 2022? 

Key figures above are based on tax and statutory deduction changes implemented during the current administration, including: 
a) The 1.5% Affordable Housing Levy.
b) 2.75% SHIF contribution
c) Doubling of fuel VAT from 8% to 16%. d) Increase of Turnover Tax from 1% to 3%. 
e) Introduction of higher PAYE bands up to 35%.

Returned at the hospital at 630pm from the Communities of Kanungu with Dan Alex a Community Nurse @BwindiHospital.

We explored 3 villages, visited 10 at risk families, referred 2, found 2 cured of Malnutrition after 1 months discharge and offered Nutrition Education to households.

Malnutrition is beyond a hospital,  it's individual,  household & Community- having no malnourished children doesn't mean children aren't there, there is always hindrance factors.

Moving to communities,  empowers them, helps identify where the problem lies and necessary Nutrition support. 

@UNICEFUganda @kiuvarsity @VUKampala @WorldVisionUg @WADiocese @WHOUganda @MinofHealthUG @DianaAtwine @JaneRuth_Aceng @NuwatuhaProssy @philbertniwae @v8_real @FrostProUg

Tweet_Text:
Nurses & Midwives in Uganda Comprise of 75% of total health workers and only 25% are other health teams.

Whereas Nurses occupy from HC11 to National and super specialised hospitals, they still lead the patient awareness and Education Sessions- empower communities and show care thus reducing the disease Burden in  Uganda.

Nurses in Uganda have gained competence, qualifications and mastered in different areas of study where Nurses are trained from Certificate, Diploma, Degrees, Master and PhDs.

Whereas graduate Nurses are common in Uganda, the public service system hasn't absorbed 5% of them.

According to Mr Patrick Odongo the Vice President for Graduate Nurses in the Country,  he says that the Ministry of Health of Uganda should emphasise the specialists in Nursing like Palliative Care, Oncology, ICU, Reproductive Health, Research among others.

He emphasises the public to embrace the Nurses not ordinary but extra ordinary Nurses in Uganda.

Every Wednesday 8-9pm at Gugudde Television., @unsbwindi

Hey guys, been long since I talked to you about the dinner! I’ve got something exciting to share, so grab a seat and let’s chat about this classy event coming up. The Nurses' Annual Dinner is back, and it’s going to be one for the books. Did you know that the first dinner was organized way back in 2012 by the founding members of UCUNSA? Yep, those trailblazers set the stage for us, and it’s been ages since we’ve had one like this. This year, we’re bringing it back with a bang, and it promises to be truly memorable in every way.

Picture this: the elegant Fairway Boutique Hotel, a venue that screams elegance from the moment you step in. The warm glow of candles, clinking wine glasses, and a sophisticated vibe that’ll make you feel like royalty. That’s where we’re hosting this dinner on 11th October 2025. 

The theme, “Beyond the Bedside: Celebrating the Leadership in Nursing Practice,” isn’t just fancy words, it’s a tribute to the incredible leadership in our nursing community. We’re not just here to eat; we’re here to tujaguza who we are and what we do.

Now, let’s talk details, fam. Tickets are up for grabs, whether you’re flying solo, rolling with your better half, repping the alumni crew, or bringing your squad:
- Singles: 130K  
- Couples: 230K  
- Alumni: 140K  
- Group of 4: 480K  

All you’ve got to do is send your payment to Bank of Africa, Account No. 06673800001 (UCU Nursing Students’ Association), then hit up Solomon Reyven (0706155623/0771039957) or Mutebi Remmigious (0756949303) to confirm your booking. Easy, right? Don’t sleep on this, secure your spot quickly and now.

This isn’t just any dinner, guys. It’s an evening of fine dining, great company, and vibes that’ll leave you buzzing. Imagine dressing up, stepping into a night of okujaguza, and connecting with fellow nurses who’ve been holding it down. We’ve got history to honor and new memories to make. So, tusanyukile  this moment together, let’s go and make it a night to remember.

We can’t wait to see you there, rocking your best fit and ready to celebrate the nursing profession in style. Tukyakalile wamu, let’s celebrate as one. See you at Fairway Boutique Hotel on the 11th.

While at @mengo_hospital ’s Dialysis Unit, I saw more than machines and hospital beds. I saw courage. I saw exhausted families holding onto hope. I saw young nurses fighting every day to keep strangers alive.

Since 2022, this @KampalaSouth supported unit has become one of Uganda’s most affordable dialysis centers, giving hundreds a second chance at life. But the need is growing faster than the support available.

That is why I challenged members of @KampalaSouth to go beyond themselves, to rally friends, partners, and every willing hand because some battles in our communities cannot wait.

To kickstart the expansion toward a modern 10-bed dialysis unit, I committed UGX 5 million through the  Kitakule Foundation.

The need is urgent. But together, so is our response. 

#RotaryEyamba

On Tuesday, participants of the Prisons Intermediate Command and Staff Course embarked on an informative study tour to key government installations, broadening their knowledge and expertise.

The day began with a visit to the National Referral Hospital, Mulago, where participants gained insight into the healthcare service provisions and infrastructure. They learned about the hospital's specialized departments, emergency response systems, and patient care management. This exposure is essential for senior prison officers, as they often oversee healthcare services of staff and inmates within various correctional facilities.

The group then proceeded to the National Agricultural Research Organisation (NARO) at Namulonge, which houses the National Livestock Resources Research Institute (NaLIRRI) and National Crops Resources Research Institute (NaCRRI). Here, they acquired knowledge on agricultural research, livestock management, and crop development. Participants learned about innovative farming techniques, disease management, and food security strategies. This knowledge will enable them to develop sustainable agricultural programs within prisons, promoting food self-sufficiency and rehabilitation through vocational skilling of Inmates.

By exploring various sectors, the course participants can develop innovative solutions to address challenges within the service through strategic planning.

Dr. Joseph Mary Sembatya presented a comprehensive overview of neurological services at Mbarara Regional Referral Hospital (MRRH), highlighting:

Key Findings:
1. High burden and diversity of neurosurgical cases: MRRH faces an overwhelming volume of complex neurosurgical cases.
2. Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI) prevalence: TBI dominated both admissions and surgeries.
3. Pediatric cases: One-third of admitted cases were children.
4. Mortality rate: 12.7%, comparable to regional institutions (12-15%).

Recommendations:
1. Raise awareness: Inform the community that safe and reliable neurosurgical services are available.
2. Scale up services: Equip all regional hospitals, except Mbarara, to provide neurosurgical services.

Dr. Joseph Mary Sembatya's presentation emphasized the need for improved access to neurosurgical services and community awareness to address the significant burden of neurological cases in the region. #ASOUConference2025

Prof. David Kitya, a renowned neurosurgeon and Professor of Neurosurgery at Mbarara Regional Referral Hospital (MRRH), expressed gratitude to the leadership of the Association of Surgeons of Uganda (ASOU) and the ASOU-ASSC 2025 committee . He also thanked the Ministry of Health (MoH) for promising to provide consumables for neurosurgery services in Uganda, a commitment conveyed by Hon. @DrAyumeCharles , MP for Koboko, who represented the Speaker of Parliament. 

Prof. David Kitya emphasized the challenges posed by shortages of consumables, stating, "We all struggle when there is a shortage of consumables - the patients suffer, so do the attendants and the surgical team."

Despite these challenges, Prof. Davis Kitya noted positive developments, including the @MinofHealthUG's approval of two posts for resident surgeons at district hospitals. This move is expected to enhance training, recruitment, and the eventual expansion of neurosurgery services to the Northern region of Uganda.

In Eastern Uganda, private institutions are providing quality neurosurgical care, but Prof. David Kitya stressed the need to bolster public healthcare services in this area.

To improve neurosurgical training, Prof. David Kitya recommended:

- Enhanced Elective Rotations: Exposure to stellar neurosurgical institutions offering specialized services in Skull base and Cerebrovascular surgery.
- Curriculum Development: Integration of surgery and neurosurgery training programs.
- Attitudinal Shift: Fostering a proactive approach to managing trauma and neurotrauma among candidates and residents. #ASOUConference

HEALTH SECTOR

·      Out of 34 Sub-Counties in Ntungamo including the Municipality, 1 has a hospital, 4 have HCIVs and 23 have HCIIIs. Therefore, 06 Sub-Counties do not have a Health Facility of either a HCIII, HCIV or a Hospital.
 
 Planned Intervention:
 
·      Upgrade Nyabushenyi HCII to HCIII in Nyabushenyi Sub-County.
·      Upgrade Kiyora HCII to HCIII in Kiyora Sub-County.
·      Upgrade Kibeho HCII to HCIII in Rweikiniro Sub-County.
·      Upgrade Kyafora HCII to HCIII in Rugarama North Sub-County.
·      Upgrade Rwamabondo HCII to HCIII in Rwamabondo Sub-County.
·      Construct a new HCIII in Nyekyera Sub-County. 
 
WATER SECTOR
 
Out of 1,033 villages in Ntungamo District, 646 have access to a safe water source representing an average coverage of 63%. 387 villages (37%) don’t have safe water sources.
 
Completed projects
·      Constructed Ntungamo Municipality piped water system serving 19,400 people.
 
·      Constructed Kagarama Town Council piped water system serving 50,627 people.
 
·      Constructed Rwashamaire Town piped water system serving 12,388 people.
 
·      Rubare TC piped water system constructed serving 4,143 people (NWSC).
 
Additional systems serving Kahama, Rwera, Buhanama, Kishami RGC, Nyamukana, Rwoho, Rwentobo-Rwahi, Kinyamufura, Kafunjo-Mirama, and Kitwe have been constructed. 
 
·      Rehabilitated of Itojo GFS and Kanyara GFS restoring service to over 1,500 people.
 
·      Constructed of Bunutsya Mini Solar Powered Water System in Ngoma sub county serving 1,800 persons.
 
·      Constructed Ishanje and Kabingo Solar Powered Water Systems in Rweikiniro and Ngoma sub counties
 
·      Constructed and installed 18 rainwater harvesting tanks in Rukanga P/S, Kishami HC III, St. Peter’s Voc SS, Ihuriro COU, St. Peter’s Nyamurindira COU, Rukoni West (Kitwe TC), Kyarwehunde, Rubaare SS, Murisa SS, Kitembe II P/S, Kyentama COU, Kibatsi H/S, Ihunga P/S, Ruhaara P/S, Karama P/S, Kibingo P/S, Kacerere P/S and Nyanga HC III.
 
·      Drilled of 4 Production Wells (kyamuhunga, kizinga, Kyabakonjo, Kigaga). 
 
·      Developed Kagorogoro GFS Designs. 
 
·      Rehabilitated 52 boreholes. 
 
*Ongoing and Planned projects*
 
·      Development and construction of Kakyera and Nyamunuka Piped Water Supply and Sanitation Systems.
 
·      Extension of Omushenyi, Nyabicence, and Kanyerere piped water supply systems.
 
·      13 new spring protections in Nyakasa, Kitagata, Nyakasa II, Nyamirama, Ntungamo, Kyamatagi, Nyakigifu, Kihorobo, Kakanena, Nyakaina, Kanyakungu, Kashasha and Nyakabare villages.
 
·      3 rainwater harvesting tanks in Kalisa P/S, Nyamatete P/S, and Kataraka SS)
 
·      Rehabilitation of 10 boreholes in Rwamwire, Nyamurindira I, Rwembogo, Nyamurindira II, Kangarame, Kyeizinga, Mutanoga, Kihorobo, Rujumo and Kagongi villages.
 
Water for Production
 
Completed projects
 
·      Constructed Kyamate Small Scale Irrigation Scheme in Southern Division on 15 acres.
 
·      Constructed 1 Irrigation System in Kabumba Sub-County,   Ruganda Parish on 9.1 acres.
 
·      Constructed 1 Irrigation System in Kitsyama Sub-County,   Nyamukana Parish.
 
·      Constructed 1  Irrigation System in Muheta Sub-County, Ruhara Parish on 14 acres. 
 
·      Constructed Rweshande  demonstration site in Nyamunuka Town Council, Iterero Parish on 11 acres with 12 beneficiaries.
 
·      Constructed Kyamate demonstration site in the Southern division, Kyamate    Parish on 20 acres with 68 beneficiaries. 

ENERGY SECTOR
 
Ntungamo District is now connected to the National Grid.

·      Out of 34 Sub-Counties, 33 are connected to electricity.
 
·    In the next kisanja, government will extend electricity to Rugarama North. #ProtectingTheGains ntu

Tweet_Text:
(Thread 3/3)

WAY FORWARD UNDER HEALTH

• Continue with immunization programmes to prevent diseases.
 
• Upgrade Tiriri HCIV to a General Hospital.
 
• Upgrade Kamuda HCIII to HCIV.
 
• Upgrade Aukot HCII in Aukot Sub-County to a HCIII.
 
• Upgrade Awaliwal HCII in Awaliwal Sub-County to a HCIII.
 
• Upgrade Lalle HCII in Lalle Sub-County to a HCIII.
 
• Upgrade Ojom HCII in Ojom Sub-County to a HCIII.
 
• Upgrade Ocokican HCII in Ocokican Sub-County to a HCIII.
 
• Construct new HCIII in Tubur Sub-County.
 
• Constructing and equipping the Regional Blood Bank and four Unit staff house at Arapai Site in Soroti City.
 
• Construction of Administration Block at Soroti Regional Referral Hospital.
 
• Renovate and expand Soroti Regional Referral Hospital.
 
• Work towards total eradication of malaria.
 
• Use ICT to track medicines in Government Health Units and to fight absenteeism of Health Workers.
 
• Construction of houses for Health Workers.
 
• Ensure that all Government Health Units are well equipped with diagnostic and other equipment.
 
WATER SECTOR
  
• Out of 359 rural villages in Soroti District, 263 have access to asafe water source, representing an average coverage of 73%. This leaves 96 villages (26.7%) without access to safe water.
 
Urban Water Supply
 
• The Soroti Municipality Piped Water Supply and Sanitation Project, now serving 83,615 people, with management by the National Water and Sewerage Corporation (NWSC).
 
• The Gweri Piped Water Supply and Sanitation Project, serving 7,521 people, managed by the Umbrella of Water and Sanitation - East.
 
Public and institutional sanitation facilities have been successfully developed in both Gweri and Soroti City, further enhancing community health and hygiene.
 
 Ongoing Efforts: Building on a Strong Foundation
 
The district continues to forge ahead with dynamic projects to increase water access and improve infrastructure:
 
• Drilling of 9 new deep boreholes is in progress to expand water availability.
 
• Rehabilitation of 11 boreholes ensures that existing water points remain functional and sustainable.
 
• Construction of 01 piped water system is underway to serve more communities with reliable water supply.
 
• Notably, work is progressing in Amodoima/Omugenya, Opucet Parish, Gweri Sub-County, where a solar-powered water source is being developed to provide sustainable and stable water supply to multiple surrounding villages.
 
Future Plans: Expanding Coverage for a Brighter Tomorrow
 
Looking forward, the government has ambitious plans to further improve water and sanitation services in Soroti District:
 
• The development and construction of water supply and sanitation systems in Tubur and Kamuda Towns will provide more communities with access to clean and safe water.
 
• The expansion of the Gweri Piped Water Supply System will increase water coverage and improve service delivery to even more residents.
 
• Construct water supply system of Lake Kyoga multi-purpose gravity flow water supply system and development Project Phase I in Soroti.
 
WATER FOR PRODUCTION
 
Completed Irrigation and Water Interventions 
 
Soroti District:
 
Successfully completed four irrigation projects, including Opiyai (15acres), Awoja (13 acres), Ojikai (10 acres), and Alele SPIS in Arapai Sub-County.
 
• Benefits:
1. A total of 166 beneficiaries supported across two sites.
2. ⁠The Opiyai site supported 135 fish farmers, boosting aquaculture production.
3. ⁠The Alere site supported 31 individuals with agricultural services.
 
Prepared feasibility studies and Designs for two (2) Valley Tanks in Soroti District with each having water storage capacity of 30 millionlitres; these sites include Tukum site in Gweri Sub-County and Akakai site in Arapai Sub-County.

ENERGY SECTOR
 
Soroti District and Soroti City are now connected to the National Grid.
 
• All the Sub-Counties of both Soroti District and Soroti City are electrified.

 #ProtectingTheGains

Tweet_Text:
What you need to know about Diabetes
 
Diabetes is a chronic metabolic disease characterized by high blood sugar (glucose) levels, occurring when the pancreas does not produce enough insulin or the body can not effectively use the insulin it makes 

This prevents cells from absorbing glucose for energy, causing sugar to accumulate in the blood. It is manageable with lifestyle changes and medication. 

Diabetes cases in Mulago Hospital have shown a rapid, consistent increase, over 100 patients attend to the clinic every Friday.

Tweet_Text:
The ongoing RR Eye Camp has gained momentum as thousands of patients continue to flock Bukedea Teaching Hospital Grounds in Bukedea District to receive free eye care services.

 The initiative, organized by the Ruparelia Group in memory of the late Rajiv Ruparelia, has not only drawn overwhelming numbers but also attracted strong praise from organizers, partners, and local leaders who addressed the media on its progress and impact.

Delivering detailed remarks, Sheena Ruparelia on behalf of Ruparelia Foundation described this year’s camp as a major milestone in the evolution of the initiative. 
“We’ve arranged an eye camp to screen individuals for cataracts, for general eye issues, as well as for prescription and reading glasses,” she said.

 She emphasized that this fourth edition goes beyond previous ones, noting, “This eye camp is a bit more special than the ones that we’ve done previously. It’s actually our fourth eye camp, and this year we’ve introduced not only surgeries for children, but also prescription glasses for children.” 

She explained the added complexity involved, stating that children’s prescriptions must be individually processed in Kampala. “It’s a big milestone for us, because we have to actually physically send the glasses back to Kampala to individualize their prescriptions for each individual child.”

According to organizers this camp has costed over Ugx2 billion including the most expensive surgeries, glasses and other much needed medical services.
#RREyeCampBukedea #RupareliaFoundation
